To her surprise, the whole area centered on the 'tree' has been reconstructed by the incantation, but she fails to find the caster within the tree's core, among its roots.
Only the roots of the 'tree' are breathing, just as always.
Please verify your credentials.
Nasti Lunorey. Director of Engineering, Rhine Lab.
Nasti dashes towards the roots. She must confirm if any abnormal changes have happened while she was away.
This is what is left of the Vivarium, and the secret Rhine Lab has been protecting at all costs. No matter how the unrest in the park ends, it is the only bargaining chip in Nasti's hand.
She touches the roots, allowing data to flow into her fingertips and gather in the 'branches' around her waist.
Muelsyse always teased Nasti about being a human computer, and she always took it as praise.
In the current situation where biotechnology has fused with Banshee witchcraft, her body is perhaps the best data cable to use.

The 'branches' begin to vibrate quickly, a sign of the device overclocking. Almost simultaneously, Nasti feels some sort of temperature shift—not the chill of condensation, not the heat of electricity, but...
Body heat?

She experiences a strange feeling out of nowhere. Here and now, she is no longer Director of Rhine Lab's Engineering Section checking if a device is still operating normally, but a Banshee touching another of her kind...
Just like so many years ago, when that woman held her own hand and taught her how to blow a leaf whistle.
Nasti abruptly looks up—
(Pained murmurs)
(Elated murmurs)
Nasti, my child...
You're back.
Report.
All pods on the levitation layer are now connected to the detonation web. We have people on the lower levels evacuating people from the square.
People are very passionate about the exhibition. Things will take some time, but it will not delay our plans.
We will detonate the levitation layer as soon as Propeller Paradise docks with the District.
Any movement from Rhine?
We've taken twenty-three into custody. A dozen are still at large on the platforms.
We have control of the eight observation points. Those people are going in and out of the exhibit area and the ropeway stations. No special Rhine equipment other than standard maintenance gear.
They're just playing hide-and-seek? No plan B?
Keep an eye on them. They don't know the details of Maylander's plan, nor do they have any means of communication. Any last-gasp effort is doomed to fail.
Understood.
The outline of Max, D.C.—the most vibrant nomadic city in Columbia—is already visible from the ropeway station's waiting area.
The agents have not had a chance to enjoy the view of Propeller Paradise until now. With an excellent visability, all the elaborate facilities of the levitation layer are brimming with vitality.
Pods float in mid-air like aquatic creatures. Floating botanical units line up orderly between small platforms, forming a power grid that supports the entire layer...
Wait... where did you say Rhine's people were?
Exhibit area and ropeway stations.
Where exactly? Show me.
Looking at their movements, there's no way they can reach the platform where that 'tree' is, or other companies' pods...
Could it be... they're not trying to take back their turf at all?
But Rhine's people, the testers, and the company reps, they all seem to be flying around blindly in the dark...
What's the point of it all?
The agent casts a casual glance at the nearest planting unit. The unremarkable Rhine creation stands quietly.
He suddenly realizes that the warning lights on their edges are easily visible even during the day, and they have been blinking irregularly for quite a while now.
Spotters report that all the Rhine people have disappeared from sight!
No, they didn't disappear!
They're going in the same direction... there's a pattern to their movement!
Everybody, listen up! Destroy all the planting units you see along the way! They're not decorations! Rhine is using them to communicate!
Cut off the exhibit area from all the ropeway stations, and find every one of those people playing hide-and-seek! Find out where they're heading!
What on Terra are they thinking...
You've been looking outside the window for a while.
You said you must win your gamble, and you have a reason.
Yes.
I'm in the middle of a gamble too.
I have a rough idea what's going on in Propeller Paradise, given the look on your face and the freefall that we just experienced.
You wouldn't run this kind of risk with me for no reason. But there's nothing I can offer you now, except—
Hm...
You want the Lucky Cystybeast?
You could say that.
I want the contraband that Gordon Tannen sold you.
Coming to the broadcast room to bother me, when you should be preparing your own speech? You sure have a thick hide, Mr. Tannen.
I would have died a long time ago if all my enemies refused to work with me ever again.
Work with you? I'm afraid I can't.
Maylander has a laundry list of violations that I committed during my career. I don't want to spend the rest of my days giving prison tours.
Neither are you looking for a peaceful life. Besides, my proposal has something to do with Maylander.
There's no better vantage point than here on Propeller Paradise. Oakley's too excited to notice, but I doubt you would miss what's going on.
Well, if I gotta say it...

The broadcast screens of the levitation layer are switching too quickly. They say it's because they're doing testing, which I'm not convinced about. There are a dozen testers on the square whom I don't recognize. They don't look like they're taking a stroll; they look like they're patrolling.
Like they know that danger is impending.
Are you trying to trick me into another plot?
There's a plot afoot on Propeller Paradise alright, that's a fact. It's also a fact that I won't let Gustave make a mockery out of this place in front of all of Columbia.
You have ten seconds. Yes or no.
What are the chances of success?
Ten percent.
Doesn't sound like a gamble you'd take.
I don't have a choice with that old fox sitting across the table, holding all the cards.
A plan almost certain to fail... interesting. Tell me about it.
I need you to convince as many people as possible to return to the levitation layer, and retrieve the Originium equipment and Arts units on this list.
...All contraband that you sold?
They have far more potential than just sitting under the spotlight.
You want to...
Escape. And take all of Propeller Paradise with me.
Gustave and Maylander never intended to allow the exhibition to go ahead. The opening ceremony was just a smokescreen.
The levitation layer will fall a second time soon. But miracles don't happen a second time.
The Lucky Cystybeast can't afford to start over...
Neither can most startups on Propeller Paradise.
'Escape. And take all of Propeller Paradise...'
It's the only way to save both Propeller Paradise and the people on the lower level.
Propeller Paradise has been traveling on the barrenlands for seven days. Fuel is short, and the plate is operating in safe mode. The basic propulsion module is not enough to change its direction.
But the Arts unit that the Lucky Cystybeast got was just a transformation stabilizer. It can't supply enough energy to turn a plate.
Correct.
But it wasn't the only item that Tannen smuggled.
You mean, get all the contraband devices, and build a makeshift propulsion module to make the plate deviate from its course?
Not deviate. Make an about-face.
About-face? Have you any idea what you're saying? It's the direction of the borders!
True, the procedures for cross-border travel is a little complicated.
It would be treason!
No, I can't go to jail. People need to see Lucky Cystybeast on the broadcasts. My wife in Northville needs to see it...
In the interest of full disclosure, I must inform you that people won't see Lucky Cystybeast on the broadcasts, whether we succeed or fail.
No...
I'm sorry, but I'm taking the unit, whether you give it up voluntarily or not. We don't have time.
What's the point? We can't even leave the pod...
Someone has unlocked it remotely.
What?
You see the floating botanical units outside the window? Three flashes is a distress call. The current message is cycled after five continuous seconds of being lit.
Right now... three, four, five... seven flashes.
Looks like the other units on the list have been retrieved. Everyone is rendezvousing on Platform 7. Wait—
...The light went out.
What does that mean?
It means Maylander knows what's up. We're in danger.
Mr. Aspen, you can either come with me on your feet, or you can go to sleep right here, right now.
People like us never get to choose, do we?
Sit down.
Gustave looks at the Savra who has butted heads with him for seven years, who now sits stiffly on a chair, like a potato wrapped in foil.
He knows young officers well. They are angry, flamboyant, unhappy about everything, and frustrated about being restricted by rules.
In the end, however, they all sit in the same chair that rectifies their young spine, and become a proper member of the upper stratum of Columbian society.
As Gordon Tannen will.
Once Maylander's plan succeeds, the broadcast room will switch over to Cloud. Don't screw up.
Put your usual attitude away. Sound sincere, devastated. It's the Propeller Paradise you built. It won't be hard to stir the appropriate emotions.
Tomorrow, that sorry face of yours is going to be on the front page of every Columbian newspaper. Look the part, won't you?
And stop staring at your little freak.
I know, I know. You don't want to be Propeller Paradise's sin eater, sharing the same spotlight as Nasti.
But your little hotchpotch trinket isn't going to save you.
So I'm sitting in Gordon Tannen's defendant seat.
Yes.
I can follow you to the DOD once I apologize, according to the script?
Do as you're told, and you'll rise quickly through the ranks.
Yeah, guess what? I don't give a shit.
What was that?
I don't like this seat. It makes my scales itch.
...Broadcast countdown. Cloud restaurant, stand by...
Broadcast? Now?
Impossible... no movement on the levitation layer. Did Maylander screw up?
Who's operating the broadcast?!
Three, two, one—
Do it, Five-Four.
Yes, sir!
What?
Ugh—
As the broadcast cuts to the Cloud restaurant, Space Fin 054 leaps at an unprepared Gustave, pinning him to the ground.
The screen in the restaurant lights up, and Gustave sees his own face, writhing in pain and shock.
He knows that this face is being displayed on every screen in Propeller Paradise.

His contingency plans flash before his eyes, but Maylander's plan is in its final phase, leaving little room for distractions. His communicator lies in pieces on the ground, destroyed by Space Fin 054.
Gordon stretches his neck and wrists, crushes the 'defendant seat' with his foot, and leisurely lies down on the sofa next to it.
Oh, that felt good—
By the way, tomorrow, that sorry face of yours is gonna be on the front page of every Columbian newspaper. Look the part, won't you?
You—
What, you didn't expect to be immobilized by a little 'hobby made to fuel sky fever'?

You're the elder statesman of ATAO, planning a new chapter at the Department of Defense. You made the rules of Columbia when you were young, and you continue to 'serve your country' even after your retirement...
Woo-hoo. First-rate, ain't you? Maybe you've grown into thinking that your enemies will always use the rules to fight back, in smart and clever ways.
Did you forget that Gordon Tannen is a bandit who never gives a shit about the rules, a piece of trash obsessed with his toys?
I guess I shouldn't be surprised. You don't even recognize Five-Four, even though I didn't change its voice module—

Hello, Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Arts Units, and Originium, at your service. To check the progress of an application, press 1. To report a safety risk, press 2. To be connected to an operator, press 0...
...An ATAO answering machine?
I said Space Fin 054 wasn't a rich man's cigar. And I won't be smug just because it obeys the rules I set down.
Seven years ago, I sat on a tiny chair that made my ass hurt, writing my first self-reflection letter late at night, wondering if I should quit.
Answering Machine 054 started giving off an unusual noise, and I racked my brains trying to figure out the problem.
I started studying every Arts unit and Originium device that passed through my hands, just to give Five-Four the ability to operate, communicate, and think on its own.
You think the hundreds of self-reflection letters I wrote were the symbol of my submission. But Five-Four is the real culmination of my seven years at ATAO!
You're full of crap! You didn't learn the tricks of bureaucracy for your little invention!
You think I've never seen you negotiate or threaten? Arrogant, vain, ambitious... I know them when I see it.
I can hear shadows of my own speeches even now as you're talking to me.
...I never thought I was perfect.
Maybe I will never be able to get rid of the bad habits that I learned from you. They disgust me, but they weren't mistakes.
My only mistake was to define my transactions as 'smuggling'. I shouldn't have allowed myself to accept the rules that have taken form, rules that don't make sense.
Foolish boy, you don't understand what's at stake—
Listen, old slug.
I don't care what high-and-mighty reasons you have. Propeller Paradise isn't supposed to be a breeding ground of war.
Sure, kinetic engines and high-powered Arts unit could become powerful weapons, but what they're supporting now is the dream of life in the sky.
Until Propeller Paradise docks with the District, I'm its chief executive, the man who built this little piece of heaven that still has a semblance of freedom—
I will decide whether it should be destroyed, or whether it should escape.
Take his passcode, Five-Four.
Yes, sir.
It's no use. The central control console is locked. You can't stop the plate from docking with the District, even if you have my passcode.
Who said anything about stopping?
What...?
I said, 'escape'.
Not only did the former head of ATAO not disable his passcode when he retired, he used the security access it granted to disable the urban identification code system on the Columbian border...
Cool, isn't it?
You're going to commit treason for this stupid, pointless children's playground?
Hah. You were right. You didn't learn a single thing from me.
Let me remind you that Propeller Paradise is running out of fuel. You can't operate the plate's steering system, and you don't have enough energy.
Even when you're pinned to the ground by the robot I built, you somehow manage to forget that I've always been an inventor.
I hope they deliver the goods on time.
The sound of rotary motors has never been so clear.
The skies are clear. Overly-pristine self-cleaning artificial clouds combine with natural clouds to form a wondrous scene, but the people on the platforms are oblivious to it.
Some fifty Propeller Paradise guards and Maylander agents block off every passage and cable connections, forming an impregnable cordon line with their bodies and weapons.
Beneath their feet, the classical black-and-white tiles form a chessboard, the two sides separated by a ten-meter-long open-air passage.
The eyes of the agents are fixed on the support platform on the other side of the passage. The Rhine staff and testers who had at one point disappeared from sight now assemble there.
Platform 7... the analysis was correct. This is where the hide-and-seek converges.
Our people are ready, but there are more of them than expected.
They took out our guys in the broadcast room. The intel was not accurate.
We're putting in everyone we have.
Strength in numbers alone isn't enough. Be on your guard. They have an advantage when it comes to the new weapons and lay of the land.
Propeller Paradise is about to dock with D.C. Do not let anyone pass.
Use force if necessary. I'll take responsibility.
Yes, sir.
Six elite squads...
We have numbers, but the guys on the other side are...
Hard to tell people to keep going when they're exhausted.
Get the word out to the others, Jelena. Anyone who's too tired to move, give someone else their core components, pick up any hover unit, and stay behind to guard the support platform.
You're betting on them being wary about unknown new products?
I hope it works.
Phew... made it.
Are you sure you want to go this way when it's like this?
Yes, this is the only way to Cloud.
Take my parachute. Things might get dangerous.
What about you?
Me?
Ferne gazes at the Maylander blockade with interest, as though she is going to meet an old rival.
The camera drones left Platform 7 half an hour ago. Nothing that is about to happen here will be seen in the broadcast room.
Everyone knows that conflict is inevitable. Neither side wants to delay.
Their eyes are fixed on each other. Everyone is waiting for a sign.
My tour is waiting for my signal.
Suddenly, a yellow-and-black warning flag is raised. A young Feline woman leaps out from the crowd, loudspeaker in hand.
The first day I snuck onto Propeller Paradise, a kind guide told me that no one in this place can be stopped, if even the sky is no longer off-limits.
From the exhibits to the underground lab, I memorized every strange and curious invention, the complex ideas behind them, and hundreds of thousands of words of descriptions.
I could tell you who made the flying toaster above our heads, its model, and the generation.
I could go on and on about how wonderful they are, but to be completely honest, I find the levitating plates less interesting than the sunset of Bleached Wasteland.
Then I remember what my father used to say to me: 'A story is always more interesting than an explanation.'
I've had many frustrating experiences in the years that I worked as an independent guide.
There's nothing wrong with seeking truth, but many people subconsciously disparage exaggeration, inspiration, and dreams yet to come true.
I swore I would never again label things that I've never seen for myself as 'impossible', and laugh at my own family with my neighbors, as I did I few years ago—
It was not until the day my father died that I truly understood what he said.
If Propeller Paradise needed a guide, I would rather tell the people of Columbia about the people I met here, about their stories.
Rice Bush.
H-Huh?
A cobbler from Bunkerhill whose dream was to leave a footprint on each of the twin moons, so that everyone who looks up with a telescope would see his shop's logo on the shoeprints.
There's no bigger billboard than the sky, after all...
Owen Fredericks.
...I'm here.
Hardware store owner who wants to put his own screw on the second Ring Lab, and earn a spot for his family when space colonization becomes reality.
Hey, never hurts to be prepared for the future.
Aspen—
I'll tell it myself.
Aspen, grocer from Northville.
After being tricked into taking out exploitative loans, I divorced my wife and left home, so the creditors wouldn't go after my family.
My wife and I came up with the name 'Lucky Cystybeast' together. We haven't spoken to each other in two years.
She was worried sick when I left. I promised that the name 'Lucky Cystybeast' would be on TV, so that she'd know I'm safe.
Now I know that the exhibition was one big, fat lie. But I want her to at least see this red balloon with the Lucky Cystybeast logo on it, even if all the pods crash.
Aspen, and all of you.

The things we want, whether they're based in reality or just a pipe dream, whether they're the result of blind conformity or superstition... they're much more important than Maylander and Gustave think they are.
Run! Jump! Fly!
We'll overcome everything that tries to block our path and change us, without ever trying to understand us. We're the ones who'll decide Propeller Paradise's path!
Ferne dashes forward like a fowlbeast, loudly blasting her signal, as the rest of the pack follows behind, charging at the Maylander blockade.
Exhibits fly over the heads of the pushing and squeezing crowd, carrying all kinds of contraband towards the sky.
Absurd, and flying free.
Phew... finally out of the woods. Good work, guys. We just have to hang in there a little longer.
Thirty-four contraband core components.... all intact. Looks like the landing module works quite well.
My work is almost done. All that's left is to deliver this to Gordon. Hope all is well on his side.
Next—
Put it down.
Turn around.
Y-You're Gustave's science consultant? What are you doing here...?
Outside the window, the pinnacle of the D.C. Government Office and the Columbian flag are already visible.
Gordon's eyes are fixed on the bottom-left of the broadcast, as the red dot on the map gets closer and closer to the District plate.
According to the plan, the devices on the list should have been delivered to Cloud more than ten minutes ago.
He forces himself to not look at the door. Gustave is bound to notice any sign of anxiety.
The plan isn't going the way you expected it, is it?
You're calmer than I expected.
Being willing to take risks is not a bad thing, but you're not good enough at assessing risk.
You didn't have time to prepare a Plan B for your reckless Plan A. All of you will pay a heavy price if it fails.
And what's YOUR Plan B?
It's all good and well to rediscover your confidence, but never forget who you're competing with. I told you that getting carried away will cause you to overlook fatal flaws.
What do you mean...?
You're late.
I was delayed.
Your science consultant...
More than just a science consultant. I needed insurance for my plan.
The tall, dark Kuranta walks towards Gordon. The box holding thirty-four Originium devices, safely contained by protective material, seems almost weightless in his hands.
He lifts his arm and hands the box over to Gordon.
What you wanted.
Thanks.
What are you doing?
I learned a lot from you. Of course I knew what your trump card was.
A rival to provoke competition. A scientific consultant with both brains and brawn. A fair and impartial outsider to give legitimacy to the hearing and plan.
Too bad you picked the wrong guy.
Gramophone made from the tentacles of fossilized Seaborn? Decorative, or specifically functional?
Oh, it's for—
I see, automatic tuning with the Seaborn's sound-producing structures...
The grooves on the edge indicate that the designer even tested whether Seaborn adjust their biostructure based on the music. An interesting idea.
Where does this golden skeletal hand come from?
The one on the wall is from Sar—
It forms a pair with the heavily corroded one on the shelf, doesn't it?
The construction of the joints and the level of wear-and-tear indicates that it originated from the western deserts of Sargon.
Ah, this runed dagger is a familiar face. It—
They were supposed to be a pair!
I got it from a Sami snowpriest, who said a pair of lovers put a curse on it with their blood. It's driven by witchcraft, and cuts through iron like butter...
I know. The other one was once impaled in my heart.
It no longer cuts through iron like butter, but it's still good enough to carve a walking stick.
Mr. Consultant, I apologize for any offense I might have caused, and I understand your desire for recompense.
But I don't have time. I brought you here for a serious purpose.
If we take off part of the structure of the gramophone and combine it with the sensor module on the metal skeletal hand, we might create a new type of voice-guided navigation model.

That witchcraft-driven blade can serve as a low-loss needle. And here, this high-powered Originium internal combustion engine, legal only in Rim Billiton, can provide thirty times the power of standard Columbian equipment.
If only we can find a Victorian transformation stabilizer, which is no longer in production, and a Locus of Sorcery that was once seen on the Columbian barrenlands—
We'd almost have all the parts necessary to put together the initial core assembly of a nomadic plate's mobility layer.
You know what I'm planning to do?
Trying to modify an entire plate's power source with a single device is extremely risky, but not impossible.
You're not one of Gustave's guys. Who are you?
You're the one who said you don't have time.
The real question is, what do you need me to do?
Gordon glares at the Kuranta standing at the center of the collection room.
The Kuranta recognized all Gordon's collections, saw through all Gordon's thoughts, and nonchalantly revealed a glimpse into his own past, as though he had pursued death across the land...
To Gordon, the Kuranta seems to stroll through the halls of his thoughts, like a king that knows nothing, and everything.
Once again, the spikes on the Savra's tail rise up on their ends, swaying conspicuously.
This time, it is not because he is on guard, but because of the excitement of victory being in sight.
How's the stability of the power unit?
It will last no more than three days, or one day and a half, if fuel is insufficient.
Good enough.
You don't actually plan on driving it out of Columbia.
You don't think I would shit on everyone's future for a grudge?
That's what you want people to think, to allow you to protect as many people as possible.
Is that so?
Treason is a serious crime.
Pfft, you should look up my file. I've got enough charges against me as it is.
Come, help me take these stuff to the control room.

The wings of a fowlbeast, the horn of a burdenbeast, the teeth of a manglerbeast, the hide of a sandbeast, and the blood of a beckbeast. What would happen if you took something from thirty-four creatures and put it all together?
Sharp, weird, chaotic, dangerous, malicious... these are the thoughts that flash through Gordon's mind as he gazes upon the power unit he just created.
But he is also filled with excitement that threatens to drive him mad.
The plate's original power system has been turned off. The device is now connected.
You may now decide the course of Propeller Paradise.
I swore an oath when I got my transfer notice.
I would be the one who lights the fire of everything new on Propeller Paradise.
I would do everything I can to make even the stupidest ideas come true. No one would ever again feel the pain I felt at ATAO, to be forced to admit their own uselessness in rules set by others.
Now, I'm still the one who lights the fire, except I'm doing it to the engine of Propeller Paradise, and let it run into the empty wilderness.
Gordon grabs the fierce-looking lever on the power unit and takes a deep breath.
Say goodbye to Max, D.C., Propeller Paradise.
Tin Man, that Ferne is leading a group on Platform 7...
...I see it through the monitors.
Looks like Tannen got one up on Gustave at Cloud.
Hearing from the guys monitoring the Rhine tree. Nasti has broke through the blockade. Our guys are trying to follow her, but they're being blocked by something that feels like Sarkaz witchcraft.
Væla...
Send a team over and get the situation under control. I'll deal with matters on the Rhine side.
Looks like we're going to meet an old friend, Larry, Curly and Mo... a mad Banshee on the brink of death might be a bit of a headache to deal with.
As for you, Merry, you can choose to stay here, or help our colleagues outside.
You should stay a little longer.
Hey, you three...
Tin Man hears no answer from his revenant friends.
Ol' Curly is lithely waving a stick and dancing an impromptu tap dance. Big Larry lies on top of Mercia's head with tears in his eyes, recalling memories of who-knows-what. As for Lil' Mo...
He is howling and yelling, and Tin Man can hear—
He seems to think he is back in Kazdel, taking out a revolver and emptying it into the elder revenant trying to stuff him into the furnace.
My legs are numb, and I'm a little dizzy...
Perhaps you've been lax about your exoskeleton maintenance.
This brain isn't supposed to rust.
Your Arts have improved considerably, huh? You managed to drag these three into the whirlpool of memory, even though Ol' Curly had confiscated the projector.
Or was it dumb luck? Did all the memories of their overly long lives make it easier for you to manipulate?
It will take a long time to play through the movies of your lives, even at 320x speed, not to mention all the moments that need to be paused and replayed...
I apologize.
You understand that the effectiveness against me is limited.
Yes, I'm sure you'll break free of the shackles quite soon. I've read the Ho'olheyak reports... she could not fully incapacitate you, even after lopping your head off.
Therefore, all I can do is stall for as much time as possible, and delay your intervention.
You're an outstanding agent, Merry.
You've always been doing an admirable job, helping people forget what they should, be it through Arts or other means.
But this is the wrong time, and you've chosen the wrong target.
You told me not to forget my family name... I've never forgotten.
Any Selene who grew up in that old house would never forget.
The 'orphanage' of Maylander Selene, predecessor of the Foundation.
It was our 'home'. We truly considered Mr. Selene our grandfather when we were little.
Do you know what my most vivid memory of him is?
When I was eight, I came home from school one day to find the doll, that should have been in my room, spinning in the middle of the garden, watering the grass around it.
It was the only thing I had that reminded me of my mother. I don't remember her face, but I remember her thimbles gleaming under the lamp when she sewed the doll for me.
Grandpa turned it into a sprinkler without a single word to me, as though the doll was always meant to do that.
The children who grew up with me all have similar stories.
You were Grandpa's best friend. You know that, to the 'Founding Father of the Federation', there are only new things in Columbia, the youngest nation on Terra, rising out of the frontiers...
The only reason for old things to stay is to be used for pioneering.
It didn't matter if we were Victorian emigrants or Gaulish refugees, whether we were children of debtors or criminals.
Grandpa wanted us to become the children of Columbia, become Selenes. That was the only reason for our existence.
However, that's exactly why I can't accept what's happening right now, and take it for granted.
What do you mean?
I can't begin to imagine what you experienced in the long course of history. The Maylander Foundation, the president, the vice-president, they all have more things to think about...

I've seen the analyses on Kazimierzian power armor. They've reverse-engineered the technology of the Gaulish Vieux Vanguard, achieving results that surpassed the 'Transmitter' using the flesh and blood of knights.
I've read the textbooks of Leithanian 'Basic Arts Tactics' courses. It will not be a surprise if, in a decade, every citizen of Leithanien is a soldier, as is the case for Laterano.
I can understand why Maylander thinks Columbia is falling behind, and how a timely arms race could bring us back on the 'right track'.
Judging by the standards that Grandpa taught me so many times, there is nothing more efficient from the perspective of national development.
It's not that I don't understand these fallacies. Indeed, if I was asked to deal with these problems, I would draw a blank, and I would be able to do little else but cry.
But I just can't take it for granted. Not now.
If orphans like us, who had nowhere to go, could all become children of Columbia—

Then why should a mother who walked a thousand miles in search of her daughter, a merchant who put everything on the line so that his wife would not be hounded by creditors, and testers who are simply trying to earn a living...
Why should they be sacrificed for the sake of Columbia's progress?
My job is to make people forget. But this time...
You decided to pay a price for Columbia's progress. You shouldn't be allowed to forget. You should remember the lives and dreams that were part of the sacrifice.
Don't think of it as no big deal.
Nasti gazes at the indistinct figure within the winding branches. The figure of her mother.
Væla's limbs have been replaced by grey-white branches, bark climbing up her clothes and skin like scales, printing countless indistinct visages.
Glowing resin pours down like a waterfall, obscuring her face. Many desperate hands reach out from within, clutching her throat like clutching at straws.
Both the 'tree' and the person within seem almost unrecognizable.
There is only one thing that Nasti can be sure of: this was not a natural occurrence.
You merged with the tree?
Falling... I see your road back falling, collapsing. I must make sure you can go home.
My incantation could not reach the broken path, but the roots of this Roosting Tree will.
...I harmed it.
(In Sarkaz) Unravel!
Nasti's incantation goes unanswered.
The tree's design was based on the grammar of the Banshees. It remains flawed in many ways, but it has never before rejected my incantation. Yet now...
The branches continue to spread around Væla, dragging her floating silhouette towards Nasti, bit by bit.
Pease, Væla, stop... let me analyze what's happened to you.
Nasti's plea goes unanswered too.
All Væla does is ask questions, in an urgent, trembling voice.
There's no mistapple pulp outside the valley... how do you polish your feather horns, Nasti?
I never taught you incantations, but it looks like you've learned it on your own... a kind of imitation... how did you...?
And your bone pen... your... your...
That's not important, Væla. What I need to know is what happened to you here.
Not... important?
Væla is suddenly silent, and the creeping branches come to a standstill.
An instant later, they start to spread in the opposite direction, encroaching upon what is left of Væla.
The fusion is progressing?
This Roosting Tree has shared your memories with me... where you went... what you did...
It can share more... more important things.
I saw you dig for answers in the great library of the Lich for days and nights... you ruined your eyesight, but the curmudgeon Fremont kicked you out in the end.
I should have told him that you were never a foolish child. It was because of me...
I saw you bind your horns and attach fowlbeast feathers to them every day, just to sneak into the city of the Sankta to find food...
But it was bad for your horns. That's why the one on the right stopped growing, isn't it?
I saw you sleep on metal scaffolds, where there were no leaves to give you shade. You didn't built a treehouse either, just used a simple metal plate...
How could anyone sleep like that...? Do you get sick?
Nasti listens to her go on and on. The voice is distorted, barely recognizable, but the words of concern are familiar.
Væla used to say these things when they lived in Convallis.
Nasti realizes that she is not looking at a problem that needs to be solved, nor a patient who needs to be treated.
She is looking at a worried mother who needs to be understood.
Nasti lowers her bone pen.
Ferne told me how you looked like when she found you. There were burns from Arts, holes from bullets, and gashes from sandstorms all over you...
You walked the path that I once did, only harder and more painfully.
You could have died... you should have stayed in the Convallis.
There was nothing more I could do for the valley, for Laqeramaline, for the children.
You've already done so much. The lies you weaved for the sake of all the Banshees...
I knew it... they couldn't fool you.
I began to sense that something was off soon after you brought me to the Convallis.
I've kept the lie going for two hundred years. This was my greatest achievement as custodian of the valley, and my greatest sin...
Two hundred years... do you mean... that war?
Yes.
The King of Sarkaz at the time was old and foolish. It was not just the Royal Courts; even outsiders thought they could turn him into a puppet and thus control all Sarkaz.
It was already a bad year. Catastrophe raked the land once, then the alliance's guns raked it again.

The green Feline appeared on the horizon, leading a wall of steel that stretched as far as the eye could see. Which were the Steam Knights, which were the spire casters, and which were the Vieux Vanguard? I couldn't tell.
I don't know when they learned to build ships of steel, and tubes that breathed fire.
All I remember is that the towering Wendigos could only whimper as they were crushed beneath the ships, and our kin fell burning from the sky like broken kites.
No one who heard the wailing that day would ever forget it.
...The wailing of the Banshee.
We couldn't tell if we were delivering death, or delivering the dead. All we could do was wail and howl, hardly able to put together a single coherent elegy.
There were less than a thousand of us left when the allied forces withdrew from Kazdel. Half of us had lost our voices.
Because of our nature, the Banshees have never missed a single war of the Sarkaz. But such losses were unprecedented.
I survived by sheer chance and left the blood-soaked land of sorrows, trying to find a place where life still remained.
The Convallis?
The Convallis.
The vast Mist Lake separates it from the outside. The seasons come to a still in the golden woods. It is a quiet place... one that seemed like it didn't belong to the Sarkaz.
There's nothing that shouldn't belong to Sarkaz.
Yes... we believed it at the time. Me, Laqeramaline, and the new king chosen by the crown.
We believed in a new life for the Sarkaz.
Laqeramaline and I took our remaining kin into the Convallis, built a dock by Mist Lake, and built our court in the forests.
For the first time, we added symbols of life into the song of the Banshee, guided the trees to bear rich fruit, and the vines to weave themselves into bridges with incantations.
For the first time, the Banshees settled in a single place. They no longer had to sleep with only the night sky as their blanket, traveling from one death to another.
But 'tokens' from outside soon came flying in like snowflakes.
Tokens announcing death.
...Yes.
Not even Theresa and Theresis could solve the problems left behind by war overnight.
Yes. Death lingers, long after the smoke has cleared.
We made dried fruit out of our first harvest. It was barely enough for a few hundred Banshees to make it through the coldest month of winter.
But the tokens in my room... the murky blood crystals of the Vampires, tattered shrouds of the Nachzehrers, the shriveled, rosen hearts that Djaj become...
They soon piled higher, much higher than our dried fruit. I knew that if we went out to deliver the dead at that time...
We would return to our old path. But the catalyst for change was won with the lives of thousands upon thousands in war.
So you made a choice on behalf of all the Banshees.
I made the choice. I convinced Laqeramaline.
I concealed all the tokens announcing death coming from outside.
Occasionally a Banshee might leave the Convallis to deliver our kin, but I chose the people carefully and sparingly... Laqeramaline faced much criticism outside.
For a long time, we believed that we could cast aside our nature and lead a different life. If the Banshees could do it, so could all Sarkaz.
Even if the price was that the owners of the tokens would never find their way to the myriad souls.
Even the price was that the Banshees, having violated their nature, are swallowed by a lifetime of emptiness, destined to endure a long and painful journey of forgetting upon their deathbed.
Two hundred years passed. Two hundred years we had endured.
Until I met you.
...That's why I had to leave.
How many deaths did you tell the others about each month? More people die in Kazdel every day.
You could fool the others, but you knew that I knew from the beginning.
Laqeramaline was right. You were too smart, and you grew up in Kazdel... I could not have fooled you.
But I hoped you could live in the Convallis.
Not just because your mother was one of the many thousands of souls that I turned a blind eye to... not just because I owed you a happy childhood for failing to respond to the last wish in time.

But also because I hoped that the Convallis, the sanctuary that was built in the hope of seeking a new life for the Sarkaz, would at least be able to take in a child from outside after two hundred years.
I took the child who was most likely to expose my lie in, as my daughter.
You carefully kept me away from other Banshees.
Ultimately, your hopes for the Convallis fell through.
I made many mistakes in my life. But there is none that I regret more than your departure.
You were always a smart girl. I've thought about it many times... that if I had told you the truth about things, you would have understood and played along whatsoever.
If that was the case, then perhaps you wouldn't have...
No, I would have left regardless.
I remember the day we parted.
You called out to me. I didn't turn around, but I looked down at the water.
There was no wind on Mist Lake that day. The boat sailed across the water like a knife cutting through a piece of paper. I saw your face clearly, reflected in the still water.
You wept when I said I was leaving, but let out a sigh of relief... so small, so imperceptible, that perhaps you didn't even notice it yourself.
Deep down, you wanted me to leave, didn't you?
We were co-conspirators in the parting.
Haven't you realized it, Væla?
It is the Convallis, not me, that is truly your daughter.
You discovered it, entrusted the Sarkaz's future to it, and built it with pavilions and boats, fruits and cakes.
Your love for it grew as time passed.
There were two reasons why you brought me back to the Convallis: to make amends for the price you paid for it, and to verify whether it has grown the way you hoped it would.
My departure brought relief, because the one who could expose the fragile lie that ties the Convallis together left.
But my departure also brought anguish, because it proved that the valley could not carry the burden you hoped it would, even after two hundred years.
It was too fragile to accept a child who grew up in Kazdel, much less become the future abode of all Sarkaz.
Nasti, don't...
Do you know the answer, Væla?
You left the Convallis, came here, and merged with this tree, which is like the Roosting Tree, yet different... did you do it for me, for yourself, or simply out of obsession?
What are you running from, Væla?
Are you running from death, from the failure of the Convallis, or from the fact that you are dying and there is nothing else you can do for the Convallis's failure?
Tell me, help me understand, and put an end to this error.
No sudden moves.
You know, I'm sure we can talk about this like reasonable people—
All the rioters are here.
Shut them in the lower level jail. We'll hand them to the courts when we arrive in D.C.
It's almost time. Double-check your parachutes unless you want to end up in a puddle.
What? Did someone press the detonate button early?
Impossible! We're less than a kilometer from the District... Wait, the government buildings are getting smaller... we're moving away from the capital?
Looks like events are moving faster than your plans can catch up, aren't they?
You never intended to stop the explosion... you were just stalling for time for the plate to turn?
Did you think we had the means to go toe-to-toe with well-trained units?
Events moving faster than the plans can catch up...
Change of plans.
Lower level, evacuate by the planned routes. Upper level, protect the civilians around you and prepare to parachute.
Point Zero, detonate.
What's going on? The broadcast has gone dark... are the screens outside getting a signal?
You're still thinking about the broadcast?! Didn't you hear the Emergency Management Council's evacuation warning?
A serious accident has occurred in the levitation layer. All floating facilities will fall from the sky in less than ten minutes! Hurry up and get yourself to the emergency shelters!
Fall from the sky...?
Oakley looks up to see hundreds of floating pods turn into fireballs in the sky, marring the clear skies with smoke and ash.
Without the gentle touch of sunlight, the cloud-like pods turn back into chunks of metal, crashing down on the lower level.
Look out.
*cough*... that was a close call. Thanks.
You're a tester? Which company? I don't remember you...
Now is not the time for introductions, Oakley.
The streets are exposed. You risk getting hit by falling debris. You should go into an emergency shelter quickly.
A-Alright...
Falling.
Tumbling down.
Ferne's heart is beating so hard, she feels like it might escape her ribcage at any moment.
She tries her best to control her breathing. The straps on the sides of the helmet slap her face, inflicting just enough pain to fend off the fear of falling.
In an instant, the signal from the detonator travels through the connected conduits, igniting all the floating facilities at the same time. Ferne leaps from Platform 7 before it loses all power.
The falling pod streaks past Ferne like a black meteor.
The bright-colored paint is burned off the advertisements, and melted cables flail in the air, giving off a bitter smell of tar.
The whole levitation layer sways meekly in the air like a deflated balloon.
'Lucky Cystybeast', 'One-Boot', 'Beachbrella'...
Ferne watches these company logos fall together with her. Now that is a scene that rivals sunset in the Bleached Wasteland, she thinks.
Pull the parachute, Ferne!
Oops, almost forgot.
The updraft lifts the Feline's slender frame. The parachutes come together to form a massive cloud.
Did we do it?
The people on the ground don't know what's happening. It must be a shock for them to see their investments and products turn to ash...
They're safe, at least. The pieces of the levitation layer would have fallen on top of their heads if the plate didn't turn around.
Besides, they'll never find out that there were scheming and plotting to stop them from dreaming.
Where do we go next?
You could certainly go back to Northville to see your wife.
Sure. What about after that?
After that?
Let's talk about that when we get back to standing on Terra Firma and looking up at the sky.
The light of burning fire illuminates the people's faces, but there is little regret in their hearts as they gaze upon Propeller Paradise, now beyond recognition.
The remains of Propeller Paradise are scattered across the barrenlands between the District and the plate, like gifts that have been returned.
Ferne closes her eyes in relaxation, as she comes back down to earth with paradise.
The elderly Banshee said her kin were like broken kites as they fell burning from the sky in that war from two hundred years ago.
Now, though, she is like a kite that has too many strings attached, the branches and roots tugging at her as she floats in mid-air.
A kite nothing could ever ruin, as she had wished.
The Roosting Tree... that was the name I gave it. I built huts beneath it, and made it the abode of every Banshee.
But all I felt was fear when Laqeramaline brought me to its roots.
It was like a great void that could not be illuminated by the moons, by candles, or by fireflies. It was still and silent, sucking away my memory a little bit at a time...
I could smell decay in the darkness. A decay that we could not reverse. Not even the Children of Soil and Stone could.
It was a constant reminder of how pathetic the Convallis was.
At the end, Lady Theresa and Lord Theresis found a future for the Sarkaz through war and death. Meanwhile, Aefanyl continues to seek a new future for the Banshees.
But me and my valley... it is nothing more than a failed sanctuary that quietly awaits death.
And there's nothing more I can do for it.
So you ran away from the Roosting Tree, ran away from death.
Why here, though?
Everywhere outside the Convallis was the same. Grey and chaotic, impossible to navigate.
There was only one path that I remembered. Those unfamiliar names that you said... Leithanien, Laterano, Columbia...
I followed the names, followed your scent, not knowing to what end the path lead, and what I would do there... I just didn't want the tree to turn me into an empty husk.
But you chose to go into another tree.
I was filled with sorrow when I saw that you planted another Roosting Tree here.
When I felt the spread of decay... I thought you were going to repeat our mistakes.
Even if we couldn't save the Convallis's Roosting Tree... I wanted to do something for this one.
Even if all I could do was stay its decay and save you from sorrow for a while...
But when I saw it with my own eyes, I realized... there was nothing I could do for it... but it could save me.
The Roosting Tree only takes memories, but this one shares.
It shares the story of the Convallis, and of your journey... it makes me feel that I'm alive.
I'm sorry, Nasti. I had fused with the tree before I stopped the path from collapsing. Once again... I deceived you.
...That's why you're so lucid.
That's why the tree rejects my incantations. You rewrote its grammar and changed its operating logic.
It keeps you alive, and you keep it running, even though neither of you are what you used to be.
You're like a program that relies on a bug to keep running...
Bug...? Program...?
Like a withered tree that relies on a parasitic vine to remain standing.
It's not so bad, is it?
If this can keep it from withering, then I've finally done something useful, for you, for the Banshees, for Sarkaz.
I can bring it back to the Convallis... bring you back.
That would be pointless.
Why... why is the tree not responding to me?
That means it's working.

The outer casing of the device Nasti is carrying on her belt falls away with a crack. Paper tapes pour out, laying themselves out around her as though following an invisible blueprint, forming an elaborate and organized framework.
The tapes are filled with tiny holes that represent incantations, coming together to build Nasti's unique language and safeguard her unique rules, rejecting the anarchic spread of the branches.
I said I must understand, before I can put an end to this error.
Now, I do.
Nasti raises her pen and writes down a function, an incantation.
(Speaks in Sarkaz) Reconstruct.
No... the memories that the tree shared with me... they're disappearing...
Nasti... Nasti! What are you doing?!
Wiping the preloaded data in the tree. All that it shared with you was only temporary data for experimentation.
I know that you don't want to forget. But what you truly want to remember and retain are not these hotchpotch data put together for experiments.
Even if it's so...
Please, don't make the last thing I see be the sight of you destroying your life's work...
You've walked such a long road and suffered so much for it...
To you, the Convallis was your life's work. But I've never had one.
I have a goal. This tree is the means to it.
My judgment of it is not based on how much I committed to it, only whether it can achieve my goal.
You hoped that fusing with it would keep it from withering. But not withering won't make it any more useful.
Just like the Roosting Tree of the Convallis.
I don't want to be cruel, but the Roosting Tree never truly consoled the dying. It is no more than a simple phoebe tree, bent under the weight of incantations.

It's native to the genus Phoebopsis in the family of Targangils-Simaroubaceae, and its wood is commonly used for buildings in southeast Terra. I had an Elf colleague verify its nature after I joined Rhine Lab.
It became the Roosting Tree of the Convallis because you saw it as a symbol and mourned its change.
It has withered because it has reached the end of its natural lifespan.
That is... all...?
Even the colors that run through its branches is merely the result of exposure to incantations over the years.
I don't remember how it used to look anymore...
But... Nasti... if it's truly so...
Why does your tree look so much like the Roosting Tree?
Because it reminds me of you.
Just as you think of me.
Do you know why I planted this 'tree'?
I remember the day we parted.
You were calling to me, but I didn't turn back, because some words can no longer be said once you see the other person's face. But I couldn't help but look at your face reflected in the water—
I wanted to remember how you looked.
New tears flow across the lines made by old ones, as though it was a fixture on your face.
I spent more than a decade with you in the Convallis. I never saw you without tear marks on your face.
Life in the valley was good. I would have chosen the Convallis over Kazdel every time, a hundred times over. But Væla...
Was there a single moment that you weren't in pain, over the course of your two hundred years in the Convallis?
At first, I thought I could wipe away your tears and make you smile with simple methods.
You spent a whole day wiping my face with mistapple pulp.
It was only later that I learned why you were in pain.
The source of your pain was the decision, the lie.
The kin who suffered because they defied their nature; the kindred who could not return to the myriad souls because there was no one to deliver them. They were the source of your pain.
To solve your problem, I must solve the problems of the Banshees, the problems of the Sarkaz.
The answer lies outside the Convallis, outside Kazdel, in a place that I don't know.
As I indulged in such fantasies...
I saw you and I sail in the sky.
The reflected sky...
It was a calm, windless day, but suddenly the water rippled without warning, wiping away the eternal tear marks on your face. You almost looked like you were smiling.
And so, I began to think about the sky whenever I thought about how to make you smile.
Perhaps...
The sky is where we can find our sanctuary, and abode for our souls.
(Speaks in Sarkaz) The Sarkaz's promised land could also be in the sky.
All the things you did, this tree in the sky...
That's where it all began.
Access security camera image.
Falling... like a broken kite...
Looks like Maylander successfully carried out their plan. The entire capital will bear witness to the fall of Propeller Paradise.
But the tremors came from the ground plate. Did Gordon...?
Will we fall too?
Not sure. I've undone the fusion and reset the tree, restoring it to an operable state.
But the tree itself is not yet complete.
At least... save yourself, Nasti...
Three years ago, Aefanyl added a condition to my hypothesis when he delivered the myriad souls. Two years ago, a friend split the sky and showed me the path to the future.
But I'm still one step away from the answer. What is to come will depend on whether we find it.
We're in the sky, but... I don't see the answer.
Everything has fallen... they sky is empty.
Yes, the sky has always been empty.
It is we who must fill it.
For you, Væla, I don't think the answer is in the sky.
It's in you.
I can hear the roars, the hatred, the cries that surround you.
The kin I delivered, or failed to deliver... Vampires, Nachzehrers, Djaj... and Banshees.
I've wiped all the preloaded temporary experiment data from the tree.

It's your own frustration, rage and sorrow that binds you now, like every Banshee that you sent away the last two hundred years, like every Sarkaz that returned to the myriad souls since the beginning of time.
That is why our people are made empty by painful oblivion. That is why the myriad souls are always roaring, always angry, always wailing.
But if we leave behind residues of information in death, and the myriad souls are merely the collective of such residues...
Why shouldn't it be joy and song that remains?
Does our nature prevent it?
No.
To process residues of information, build a server. If coverage on the ground isn't enough, raise it into the sky.
Not just a sanctuary for the living; the 'tree' will become an abode for the departed as well. This time, however, we decide what we leave behind.
Perhaps it wasn't function that the 'tree' was missing. It was a command, a decision, a sincere wish.
Tell me the answer, Væla. What is it that you truly want to leave behind?
The tree... I will preserve that memory for you.
Oblivion will never claim what you want to leave behind.
I don't know.
You don't know...
At first, I just wanted to save our kin from pain, from anger, from sorrow.
But I no longer know how that can be achieved. If neither the Convallis nor the Roosting Tree could accomplish it... is there any meaning in leaving anything behind?
Væla...
Nasti wanted to do something, say something, but no sound comes out from her lips.
She is normally not one to deal with hypotheticals, but she could not help but think: If she finished the tree and took her people to the sanctuary in the sky sooner...
Would her mother have had a comforting memory to cling to?
Hundreds of pods fall burning from the sky, like black meteors, like heavy raindrops, like broken kites.
Mother and daughter gaze at each other silently, as they hoped it would make this moment an eternity.
No...
You were never one to stop walking, Nasti. Once you've decided on a path, you'll see it through to the end.
Don't stop now. Don't let me take away the meaning of what you've accomplished. My life may have had no meaning, but there must be some meaning to all that you've done...
If this tree is the new abode for the souls of the Sarkaz, then let me be its first denizen.
I gave you a home twenty years ago. Now, you've given me one.
When the children stand around the tree to listen to its voice... I want it to be your laughter that they hear, not your weeping.
And I want to remember your smile when I think of you...
Nasti, my child...
I've had good memories... memories that made me smile...
I remember... it was...
She cannot finish the sentence.
Half a year has passed since she realized that she was beginning to forget, and fled her hut under the Roosting Tree, following Nasti's trail all the way to Columbia.
A journey far too long for a dying Banshee.
It was not the reunion that she had hoped for. But as the obsession that carried her to this point falls apart, death has finally caught up to her.
Countless scenes fade from Væla's memories, and become vivid before Nasti's eyes.

lines.st: The Banshee's oar lies still in her hands.
She hears a lament. The king who painted the same dream with her has passed, in a faraway place that she cannot see.
The Banshee stands still beneath the Roosting Tree.
She spent three months witnessing her kin fall victim to oblivion and become an empty husk, the price they paid for her lies, and there was nothing she could do about it.
The Banshee kneels still on the floor of a dilapidated house.
She promised to deliver a Gargoyle who helped her in the past, but all that was left was the tip of a petrified tail.
The Banshee's sorrow and regrets are infinite, flowing past Nasti like a river without end.
After all of these memories have faded, there is one last thing that is left. One tiny, brief fragment.
The Banshee is putting a child to bed, a girl whom she brought back to the Convallis.
Her eyes are filled with fatigue as she looks at her old friend, but all she would talk about is how lovely the child is.
She used to be a child, once.
She opens her eyes and sees this land for the first time. She does not know whether it is good or bad.
But Nasti can see that she is smiling.

lines.x: Time passes through the Banshee, like the wind across the surface of Mist Lake, leaving nothing behind.
Væla fades away into the sky.
A leaf gently drifts down.
I'll catch you, and remember you...
Mother.
Nasti catches the leaf gently, as her mother once picked a leaf from the tree for her.
...One does not need a bone whistle to accompany a smile.
After a long silence, she blows a tune with the leaf.
Like threads, the notes weave the gentlest incantation that she has ever weaved.
Be at peace.
I'd like to make a bet with you, Miss Wilson.
How many sky stocks do you think young Pavard, that honest and serious secretary of mine, will go and buy once he delivers the balloons?
Zero. The end of the line at the stock exchange was two blocks away when I came.
The last time I saw this level of excitement in the District was when the Department of Defense decided to send troops to Bolívar. I still have one of those cactus hats in my office.
People went into the streets wearing those hats, shouting slogans and turning the veins of the D.C. green.
I haven't seen those hats in a long time.
No one wants their taxes turn into guns, not since Robert McKesson and his feat of 'saving Bolívar from the Bolívarians' became the butt of evening radio's jokes.
Everybody wears a propeller on their heads now.
A blue propeller hat that spins. It's awfully noisy, to tell the truth.
I hear there's a new version that automatically plays that rock song every Columbian knows when the propeller spins.
I grew up listening to 'Terra Bless Us' and 'Cityway 66'. I once thought that the song of our time would be 'Goodnight, Arc-01.'
Turns out it would be 'Good Night, Kristen Wright.'
The obsession that Columbians have with the future will never change, but the one they're following seems to have changed while we weren't looking.
Kristen never intended to become an idol.
The Department of Defense never intended to become a joke.
It never occurred to any of us, when we raised a glass to the failure of the DOD, that it wouldn't be just the DOD who loses credibility.
The people listening to late-night radio over a pot of mac & cheese don't differentiate between the DOD, Congress, or the cabinet, after all.
As you said yourself, these days, the masses seem more willing to take inspiration from a legend or miracle.
Sometimes, however, we need to turn their gazes back to the soil that nurtures miracle.
Back to Columbia.
Finally, we lay our eyes on Propeller Paradise. Not a day has passed that we don't think about it, ever since that fateful night two years ago...
The synchronized aerial turn that we just witnessed is a testament to the skill of our test pilots, even in times of peace.
Now, Propeller Paradise reveals its miracles to use, this wonderful spectacle of the Columbian Dream—
Demonstrating that—
Declaring that—
Silence.
Every media outlet, every channel, voices rich or coarse or sharp or warm, all fall silent at the same time.
The entrepreneurs and investors on the streets, along with thousands upon thousands of ordinary people, fall silent too.
For a few minutes, the only sound in Max, D.C. is the sound of their propeller hats.
The rustling sound they make, like falling leaves, is the only accompaniment to this spectacular disaster, witnessed by millions in the capital.
...'turn their gazes back to the soil that nurtures miracle.'
Back to Columbia.
A terrorist attack?
...A premeditated act of terror.
Investigators confirm that the explosion was caused by a batch of Arts weapons smuggled in disguised as general parts.
The weapons can be traced to numerous centrum nations, including...
Kazimierz, which has long since abandoned the ways of chivalry, and Leithanien, the ancient nation of casters, have used their despicable tricks against us.
The voice of progress falls silent today. It is the darkest day in Columbia's eighty years of history...
The nation is waiting for me to speak.
I should tell them: 'They have destroyed more than just an exhibition; they have destroyed the hopes and dreams that every Columbian entrusted to it.'
I should tell them: 'This shameless, heinous act was committed by those who envy our splendor, trying to make our people timid and afraid.
I should tell them: 'But they're doomed to fail. Columbia will never be defeated. No conspiracy can stop the Lamp and Star from shining bright in the west.'
And then, Columbians will eagerly turn their taxes into guns again?
Everything that they fervently devoted to Propeller Paradise, all those sky stocks, and the spinning propeller hats, will turn into shields and swords.
When the smoke clears, the empty sky will be more inflammatory than any speech.
The winds on the wilderness of Columbia have never stopped, mercilessly blowing away the smoke and cloud that surrounded Propeller Paradise.
The sky feels even emptier than before, after all things bright and beautiful have fallen.
But it's not entirely empty.
Is the soot playing tricks on my eyes?
I see it as well as you do, Mr. Vice President.
As do millions of people here in D.C.
Floating platforms supported by a complicated system of massive hot-air balloons and propellers have fallen. Floating labs of many shapes and sizes have fallen.

The ropeway system that connected the levitation layer has fallen, along with hundreds of pods. The saucer-shaped tower that linked the ground and the sky has fallen, along with the elevator that it supports.
Only one thing rises through the clouds, into the bright skies of Columbia.
Message from Maylander, sir.
Propeller Paradise has stopped moving toward the capital. It is now headed for the southeastern border, accelerating.
Maylander believes they are trying to escape Columbia, which would be an act of treason.
Mechanized units on the border are moving to cut off Propeller Paradise's potential routes.
More importantly, the unknown object in the sky has already been locked on by our defensive artillery deployed along the District's route.
With your speech about to begin, they want to hear how you define it.

The Department of Defense recommends opening fire immediately, and sticking with the current narrative. Maylander has raised the possibility of conveying a different message through the survival of this facility.
But they have also noted the risks of doing so. The facility was created by Rhine Lab, after all.
Before they do anything, I would like to get a good look at this thing myself.
A tree?
A tree.
Rhine certainly has style, doesn't it? A tree-shaped facility. Do you know what it is?
I have no idea. I suppose I'm just here to lighten the mood while you vent about Rhine.
Heh. Well, it doesn't matter.
What it is will soon stop mattering to the people, just like that balloon that couldn't fly.
In the DOD's game, it will become an infant slaughtered by knights, an envoy killed by the Witch King. Its fall will drive the rise of the people's wrath.
In Maylander's proposal, it will become the next symbol of the Columbian spirit, its silhouette printed on every hat that leaves the factories for the next decade.
Is that so? All I see is a tree.
You see those fowlbeasts, brushing their feathers and honing their beaks?
They've accepted that it's a tree. A tree that they can roost on.
A tree rising from the wounds of Columbia? How should people understand it?
They don't have to.
Just as you didn't need to understand the mobile mining platform on the wilderness that you skipped classes to watch when you were young.
Did you know if it was carrying a group of ambitious pioneers, or desperate debtors? Did it return with a full load of riches, or was it simply drifting aimlessly?
...Hah.
All I know is that I really didn't want to take a test that day, and the mining platform happened to be chasing the setting sun.
A mining platform chasing the setting sun, and a flying tree.
They're not so different, are they?
What is your position, Mr. Vice President?
I'm the vice president of the Columbian Union. With this position, comes responsibility.
I can't skip classes to watch a mining platform chase the setting sun, like I used to.
Still...
That was, indeed, what made me feel something about the soil of Columbia for the first time.
If the sky is the soil that nurtures the future of Columbia...
I'm sure there's enough room in it for a flying tree.
A most remarkable speech, Mr. Vice President.
People were moved to tears, even in the Rhine lounge.
I didn't think I would move the most eccentric, most maverick scientists of Columbia.
But I've received enough flowers, banners, strongly worded letters of support, and soldier figures painted in my likeness over the last few hours.
Not from Rhine.
...For the better, I suppose. Let's get back to the topic at hand.
How should we define that 'tree' floating in the sky, from a scientific perspective?
If you really want to know the scientific principles behind it, I could spend the whole afternoon explaining how its roots will spread across the land, and its branches and leaves across the sky.
From a scientific perspective, however, all you need to know is that when it is fully grown, it will be able to collect energy and information from across Columbia and process it centrally.
You mean... an energy well like the S.H.A.F.T., but on a national scale?
And a server, too. Bidirectional, to boot.
The roots and branches go both ways—not only from the ground to the sky, but also from the sky to the ground.
Haha.
No need to force yourself to understand these things. You can zone out the way you did when you visited our Trimounts headquarters.
It was your words that saved the tree when dozens of guns from the Department of Defense and hundreds of cameras from Maylander media were pointed at it.
Miss Wilson's words truly tugged at the heartstrings. Even an old man like me couldn't help but think back to the wild days of youth.
I see.
And there I was, thinking that it was because you didn't want to give away the chance to define this tree to Maylander or the Department of Defense.
An exhibition, a speech, Sky Fever and patriotism have made you the overwhelming darling of public opinion over the other candidates, in the span of just a few hours.
But have you thought about the future you would need to paint, in order to not let your supporters down?
Or was it always your plan to improvise your speech?
You know about the understanding between Kristen and the Department of Defense. Kristen would build Arc-01, and it was none of Rhine's business who the DOD used it against.
Since I already have the authority to define this tree, I don't suppose it would have broken through the Starpod before everyone's eyes, if I didn't discuss with Rhine when defining it?
Of course you don't have to discuss with anyone, be it Nasti, myself, the generals, the lawmakers, or that plushie on your shoulder.
...Hehe.
I take back what I said about you when I was chatting with my secretary.
What do you mean?
You're easier to deal with than Kristen.
State your demands.
Lift the lockdown on Propeller Paradise and allow the escaped plate to return to the capital's route for repairs. As to those involved in the incident...
'Escaped'? That's not how D.C. will define what has happened.
Researchers who stand on the front lines of Columbian science would never flee in the face of foreign sabotage, would they?
What else?
Lift the lockdown on Rhine Lab... you know what I'm talking about.
You and I should hold an official meeting as soon as possible. You can't ask the government to undo something we haven't done. We've only ever been curious...
What can Rhine Lab do?
Seriously, is no one sleeping tonight? I should have held some night wilderness tours.
Heh. You just wouldn't stay still, would you, even when the plate's broken down.
I could say the same about you. You look like a clerk gazing blankly at the sky outside after coming back from vacation.
Well... I was scared, but also excited, when we started fighting the Maylander people.
You almost scared ME when you tried to bite that agent's finger off.
I'm not really an emotional guy. The last time I was so excited was when I got approved for that Sky Loan.
The loan shark was showing off levitating devices at the entrance to the local bar. There were so many people watching that they broke the fence.
In the end, it wasn't education or economic circumstances that decided who got the loan, but who could fly a drone the highest and furthest.
Which was how you ended up being the sucker who got scammed into it.
That night, I stupidly bragged to my wife, showing off the finger I used to move the control stick and win a chance for the future...
It was only a few days later that you realized there was no battery in the controller at all.
Yeah, I should never trust myself when blood gets into my head.
But you still charged the blockade with us without a second thought, didn't you?
Yeah. Now we're like cystybeasts to the slaughter in the wilderness, waiting for the government to decide our future...
I feel like an onion thrown into a washing machine, getting layers after layers peeled off in these tides of fevers...
Do you want to escape the washing machine, or do you want to be more than a powerless onion?
Hm...
A few years ago, I had an idea while going into the Bleached Wasteland for the third time.
If I sneaked inside with a truckload of pure gold ore, and took it through the border checkpoint in broad daylight...
Columbians would forget all the terrifying legends in the blink of an eye and begin another Great Expansion, and it wouldn't be much different from what we're going through.
Even if it began with a bored tour guide rather than a scientist who split the sky; even if it was a hoax rather than a milestone.
We always try to see new possibilities inside the door marked 'impossible', to escape the laws of common sense that seem to have no end, to go somewhere else and live a different life.
You, me, the hundreds of Propeller Paradise testers, even that Rhine researcher assistant, Sky... we're all the same.
The onion can't get out of the washing machine...
But we can remember why we chose to go inside.
If the government doesn't hang us all for treason tonight, I want to stay on Propeller Paradise, run Lucky Cystybeast, and pay my debts.
Can you deliver a letter to my wife? I don't really trust regular Messengers.
Would you like to join Ferne's Tours? I could deliver YOU.
I don't believe it. There can't be a single scientist on Terra like you.
(Sound of hammering)
How did you meet Gustave? Did you know about his and Maylander's plan?
(Sound of metal being polished)
Shame I never actually got to teach him a lesson. The old fox ran off with Maylander's people after the levitation layer fell.
He was taken away in handcuffs.
Handcuffs? Ha! He wanted me to take the fall for Propeller Paradise, but he's the one who ends up going down!
(Sound of metal clanging)
Good.
You've been sitting here the whole day with those pliers and hammers... what are you studying?
I'm preparing for a journey.
The Kuranta gestures to the bottom of his 'shoes'. Only then does Gordon realize that the sound that the Kuranta makes when walking does not come from shoes.
A piece of crescent-shaped metal... nailed to your feet? Doesn't it hurt?
No. Feet are more trustworthy than shoes in treacherous terrain, such as the swamps of Sargon, the icefields of Sami, and the barrenlands of Columbia.
So you don't plan on hiding your identity from me.
My role is insignificant in this entire event.
I simply came to help a friend, and made a new friend along the way.
I do so hate free spirits.
You have enough experience to be my travel companion. But you won't do it.
ATAO's gonna be purged after this accident. Why should I not go back when the old fox no longer has any influence?
Besides, you recognized everything in my collection room. I'll always be inferior to you if I traveled with you.
I'll make good use of all ATAO resources available to me. You won't recognize Five-Four the next time you see it.
I look forward to it.
Is there any place you haven't been? Like beyond the Starpod, I guess?
There are many paths that no one has trodden before.
If there indeed comes a day when humanity stands among the clouds, I will continue to cast my gaze to the land.
I saw you coming this time, Mercia.
It's rare to see you not burying your head in work. Are you waiting for someone?
What about you? Your colleagues already pulled out of Propeller Paradise, before the sun had set.
Another rule of secretaries? You have to be the last one to leave?
Just ran into a bit of trouble when packing.
(Speaks in Sarkaz) Hover.
Oh, thank you. I was wondering how I would get them down there.
Yet you chose the most scenic route, one that would take you around the tree.
I wanted to see the final resting place of Væla... your mother.
Her epitaph is the leaves that grow upon it.
Look up on your way out. She might have the wind carry one to you, if she still remembers you.
You're not angry that I used Væla to complete Maylander's mission?
You also defied Maylander's orders in order to help her.
If it's to prison that you go from here, I would not consider it a shining example of Columbian justice.
No need to worry about that.
The Tin Man once decorated himself with drone parts that Mr. Selene discarded. Usefulness is not the only standard that he uses to judge.
And this little rebellion won't be, either.
Mm.
Ah... I think that's the one you were waiting for?
I'll take my leave, then.
Give me the parameters, Sky.
Transmission complete. Nothing out of the ordinary. The tree is in great shape.
But you already know that, don't you?
Just making sure the battle didn't wreck your brain.
The first thing I learned on the streets of Trimounts was how to fight. I wouldn't be here at Rhine if a fight or two could wreck my brain.
Until I come back, you're in charge of the tree's regular maintenance, any derivative projects, and all our staff, workers and testers in Propeller Paradise.
I need you on top of your game.
I got the projects, but the workers and testers... they have been asking what you liked. They wanted to give you a token of their appreciation before you leave.
They stayed because of you, Director Lunorey.
What did you tell them?
Uh... I don't know. I have no idea what you like... other than our work.
Take a Starpod Wars stamp to Pitt tomorrow. He'll tell you everyone's personality and interests.
Director...
The first thing I learned on the barrenlands was how to deal with people.
What do you like, then?
It's not hard to find out.
It's time to go. I'll leave the tree in your hands.
Huh? What about the one you were waiting for?
He's used to traveling alone. He's probably already left.
Left? But we're in the barrenlands!
He'll find a way.
Hey, stop. This is Rhine Lab HQ. Visitors need to register over there.

I noticed you the moment you walked in. Hard not to, given the clatter you make when walking. Did you just come back from the barrenlands or something? At least change your clothes, if you want to steal company secrets.
What the... director-level access? Who are you?
I-I'm sorry. I'll take you upstairs now. The others are waiting for you in the conference room.
Thank you.
...That's the gist of it. The rest of the details are on the slide.
Propeller Paradise has docked with Max, D.C.. The District will now proceed to perform repairs and take inventory.
Hundreds of tech firms, more than a thousand research projects on the sky and flight, and some twenty to thirty-thousand people... it could take months to complete.
Fortunately, Saria's negotiation with Jackson was a success, and the Rhine Engineering Section will be able to focus on their work at Propeller Paradise.
Sky Jagger is in charge until I get back. He's matured a lot.
Wow, you're actually developing the new guys.
Good work, Nasti.
Thanks for your help... I would thank you even more if you deleted the photos where you make faces with my appearance.
Nah, don't think so.
Say, aren't you gonna tell me about your mom?
No.
Something's different about you... not sure what, exactly, but in a good way.
Nasti...
Alright. I cried, I came to terms with a few things, and strengthened my resolve with a few things. That's all.
Ahem. I didn't leave my lab to watch your little tête-à-tête.
You've been a little too committed to science lately, Ferdinand. I'm almost starting to miss the days when you were scheming left and right to take over the company.
And why do you think I'm not doing that?
I'll ask you to recall that it was MY breakthroughs in high-energy physics that have kept all our sections afloat these past two years, ladies!
Yes, sure, thank you for your help.
I'm guessing Dorothy didn't come to the meeting because she didn't want to hear you gloat.
